model_narrative: We choose which of a collection of candidate columns to select, where each column may be either taken or left out. Every selected column consumes some amount of each row's limited resource and earns a known value. We also track how much capacity is left unused in each row. The objective is to maximize the total value accumulated across all selected columns.

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the set of candidate columns, that is the items or projects that may be selected; indexed by labels

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the set of rows, each row being one capacity restriction that the chosen columns must collectively respect; indexed by labels

params.name: obj_coef
params.doc: the value earned from selecting each column, in value units; one nonnegative number per column

params.name: rhs
params.doc: the available capacity of each row, that is the right-hand-side limit that the total resource usage in that row may reach, in resource units; one nonnegative number per row

params.name: a
params.doc: the amount of a row's resource consumed by selecting a given column, in resource units; defined for each row and column, and zero where a column places no demand on a row

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the selection indicator for each column; equals 1 if the column is selected and 0 otherwise

vars.name: slack
vars.doc: the unused capacity remaining in each row, in resource units; a nonnegative quantity

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total value accumulated over all selected columns, in value units

constraints.name: a[i,
constraints.intent: For each row, the total resource that the selected columns consume in that row, taken together with the capacity left unused in that row, must come out exactly to the capacity available in that row. In other words every row's consumption plus its leftover capacity accounts for the full capacity of that row.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total accumulated value must equal the combined value of all the columns that are selected. Add up the value earned from each selected column and set the total value equal to that sum.

query: We're curious how lean the program can run if we don't overcommit. Suppose we allow at most two columns to be selected in total — what value does the optimizer return under that two-pick ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.cap2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[j] for j in model.j) <= 2)

query: There's a two-part proposal on the table for de-concentrating the program. First, columns c3 and c6 are seen as overlapping bets, so the board wants at most one of that pair carried. Second, the niche column c5 should only ride along when the low-footprint column c1 is also funded. With both of those rules layered on together, what total value does the optimizer return?
expected_pyomo:
model.excl_c3c6 = Constraint(expr=model.x['c3'] + model.x['c6'] <= 1)
model.c5_only_if_c1 = Constraint(expr=model.x['c5'] <= model.x['c1'])

query: We're looking at a scenario where two niche columns, c1 and c5, both have to be carried for compliance reasons. With both of those locked into the selection, what total value does the optimizer come back with?

query: Portfolio governance wants a fixed-size program — exactly three columns funded, no more, no fewer. What total value can the model achieve if it must pick precisely three?
expected_pyomo: model.exactly3 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[j] for j in model.j) == 3)

query: Columns c3 and c6 both lean hard on the same tight resource rows, and the plan happily carries both of them at once — that feels like doubling down on one corner of the program. Shouldn't the slate keep at most one of that pair active so we're not over-concentrated? What's the model seeing that makes running both the better call?

query: Our risk team treats c3 as a follow-on bet that only makes sense once the hedge column c5 is on the books — yet the plan funds c3 while leaving c5 out. Shouldn't c3 only be allowed in the slate when c5 is carried too? What's keeping the model from pairing them that way?

query: The plan runs several of the tight resource rows almost to the wire, which leaves us no cushion if any budget gets revised down. Shouldn't every resource row hold back at least a small spare buffer of unused capacity rather than being packed to the edge? What's the model's reasoning for shaving the margins that thin?

query: We're only funding three columns and that feels thin given how much value is on the table. Couldn't we squeeze in at least four and come out ahead? What's stopping the model from carrying a fuller slate?
expected_pyomo: model.atleast4 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[j] for j in model.j) >= 4)
